FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes cross-sectional data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, Year 2 (2018-2020), ages 11-12, N=10,157, recruited from 21 U.S. study sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: multivariable logistic regression estimating associations between sociodemographic factors and online dating app use in ABCD Year 2 data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in BMC Research Notes (2024); no indication this is a duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: the DV is online dating app use ("Have you ever used a dating app?"), which is an explicit qualifying SM/phone variable (online dating apps). The paper reports AOR 12.97 (95% CI 4.32-38.96) for sexual minority vs. heterosexual identification and AOR 2.72 (95% CI 1.11-6.78) for male vs. female sex. Sexual minority status qualifies as individual-trait-level (identity variable per criteria); sex paired with online dating app use also present. Step 1 MET.

FT_IC3.evidence: Table 2 reports adjusted odds ratios with 95% CIs and p-values for all sociodemographic predictors of online dating app use (e.g., AOR 12.97 for sexual minority status, p<0.001; AOR 2.72 for male sex, p=0.03).

FT_IC4.evidence: Multivariable logistic regression with numeric AOR estimates, 95% CIs, and p-values reported throughout Table 2.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional; FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: Online dating app use is the DV; sexual minority identity (individual-trait) and sex are key predictors. IC2 MET at Step 1 via the online dating apps qualifying variable paired with sexual minority status (individual-trait identity variable).

FT_IC1.evidence: This perspective article analyzes data from 657 16- to 18-year-olds from five schools in England (Carter et al. 2024 UK study) and a prior survey of 18- to 24-year-olds. The ABCD Study is cited only in the discussion as a reference (Bagot et al. 2022, ref 7) to contextualize smartphone use patterns, but no ABCD data are analyzed by the authors of this paper.

notes: Paper is a perspective/commentary (Acta Paediatrica, labeled PERSPECTIVE) analyzing UK school data, not ABCD data. ABCD cited only in background discussion (ref 7 = Bagot et al. 2022). FT-IC1 NOT_MET; exclusion code FT-EC1.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper explicitly analyzes ABCD Study data: "We included ABCD Study participants who completed the 2-year follow-up... N=10,147, Mage=12.0... Starting in 2016, ABCD recruited diverse children (N=11,876) ages 9-10 through schools near 21 sites across the US." Data Release 4.0.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: exposome-wide association study (ExWAS) with linear mixed-effects models and logistic regression applied to ABCD data to test associations between 41 digital/SM exposure variables and youth psychopathology (BPM T-scores, CBCL, suicide attempt history).

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in NPP - Digital Psychiatry and Neuroscience (2024); no indication this is a duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: paper tests numerous phone/SM-specific variables as IVs with individual-trait mental health outcomes as DVs. Named-platform screentime variables (TikTok, Instagram, Snapchat, YouTube, Reddit, Twitter, Tumblr) and addictive SM use scale items each receive individual ExWAS LME coefficients against BPM Total Problems T-scores (e.g., addictive social media use coefficient shown in Fig. 2 / Table S1). The aggregate digital exposomic risk score (estimate=1.78, 95%CI=[1.58-1.98], t=17.41, p<0.001, Table 2) is built from these SM-specific variables. Mental health (BPM, CBCL, suicide attempts) is a qualifying individual-trait outcome. Step 1 MET.

FT_IC3.evidence: Table 2 reports LME estimates with 95% CIs and t-statistics for digital exposomic risk score vs. BPM T-scores (estimate=1.78, p<0.001). Table S3 reports OR=1.76 (95%CI=[1.39-2.23]) for suicide attempts. ExWAS Fig. 2 and Table S1 provide individual coefficients for all 35 significant SM/digital exposure variables.

FT_IC4.evidence: Quantitative results reported throughout: LME beta coefficients, odds ratios, marginal R2=15.61%, t-statistics, and p-values for SM exposure-mental health associations.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional (ABCD Year 2 data; authors explicitly state "the current analyses leveraged cross-sectional ABCD data"); FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: ExWAS approach testing 41 SM/digital exposure variables as IVs against youth psychopathology outcomes. Named-platform screentime (TikTok, Instagram, Snapchat, etc.) and addictive SM use each have individual coefficients in ExWAS results. Cross-sectional design; SM variables are IVs throughout. INCLUDE.
